nativeRange.habitat: The Pearlscale Butterflyfish is a non-migratory reef species commonly observed on clear coastal to outer reef slopes and drop-offs, typically below 15 m depth, often in association with staghorn coral thickets.

decorPreferences: fine sand or crushed coral substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on small benthic invertebrates, coral polyps, and algae. In aquaria, generally accepts a varied diet of mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, chopped krill, marine flake and pellet foods, and prepared frozen formulas containing marine algae and spirulina. Small portions offered several times daily tend to support better acclimation and long-term condition.

summary: The Pearlscale Butterflyfish is a peaceful Western Pacific species recognized by its pearly white body overlaid with a fine black crosshatch pattern and a contrasting orange-and-yellow tail. It is generally considered not reef-safe due to its tendency to nip coral polyps, and is best suited to a fish-only or FOWLR aquarium.
careNotes:
The Pearlscale Butterflyfish (Chaetodon xanthurus) is generally considered an intermediate-level saltwater fish. It tends to be docile and peaceful, and is reported to do well singly or as a bonded pair when both individuals are introduced simultaneously. Like many butterflyfishes, it has been reported to form monogamous pair bonds in the wild, so established pairs are generally advised not to be separated.

This species is commonly considered not reef-safe — in the wild it has been documented feeding on hard and soft coral polyps and small benthic invertebrates, and is therefore best housed in a fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) aquascape. Plenty of live rock arranged into caves and overhangs, along with generous open swimming space, tends to suit it well. Ornamental anemones, zoanthids, tubeworms, and clams are generally best avoided in the same system.

New arrivals are often shy and have been reported to need extra care during acclimation. Offering small amounts of varied meaty and algae-based foods several times daily tends to help them settle in.

A close lookalike, the Atoll Butterflyfish (Chaetodon mertensii), is distinguished by the white-rimmed dark spot behind the eye band that the Pearlscale shows but C. mertensii does not.
breedingNotes: The Pearlscale Butterflyfish is generally considered not bred in home aquaria. In the wild, the species has been observed forming monogamous pairs during the breeding season and is presumed to be a broadcast spawner, releasing pelagic (free-floating) eggs into the water column as other Chaetodon butterflyfishes are known to do.
